issue_title: Growth, Water Scarcity, and Long-Term Supply in Nevada
input: working/nevada/water-scarcity/evidence-pack.json (108 policy bills of 189 collected; 2019-2025; recall audit 2026-07-17 added Tahoe/rivers/flood/sewage headings and checked all special sessions)

2019.note: highest pass rate in the record

2023.note: first vetoes appear in the record

2025.note: worst pass rate in the record; most failures stopped at the first committee

theme_scorecards.label: Money for water projects
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: the three failures died early: two in a money committee (82:SB102, 83:SB159), one in first committee (81:SB73 Reno flood funding)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: appropriations and grant-program bills nearly always finish once they reach a floor; the filter is the money committee, not the vote.

theme_scorecards.label: Water permits and the State Engineer's rules
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: bigger changes stop in first committee or first floor (80:AB30 was the exception: passed Assembly 31-9, died in Senate)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: narrow, technical permit fixes often moved; bills adding new duties or conditions on the State Engineer moved less.

theme_scorecards.label: Water utilities, districts, and local agencies
Clean water and pollution
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: scattered; the constitutional clean-water right (82:AJR3) died in first committee; Tahoe cleanup-program bonds passed every session

theme_scorecards.label: Using less water
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: 2025 SB143 (water-saving review + turf study) passed the Senate 21-0 and died in the Assembly
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: conservation mandates on suppliers often moved; the record's most famous water law (81:AB356 grass ban) is here.

theme_scorecards.label: Colorado River, planning, studies, and new supply
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: asking-the-feds resolutions pass; restructuring agencies dies in first committee (80:SB76, 81:AB15); planning mandates got vetoed in 2023 (82:SB169)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: cloud seeding is the only new-supply idea that received state money in this record - twice, unanimously.

theme_scorecards.label: Groundwater, wells, and over-pumped basins
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: first committee (5 of 8 non-enactments); 82:SB180 passed both houses 41-0 and unanimous, still no law; the 2025 retry (83:AB363) died on the Assembly floor calendar

theme_scorecards.label: Buying, banking, or retiring water rights
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: failed in 2021 (81:AB354 water banking, first committee) and 2023 (82:SB176, first floor), then 83:SB36 became law in 2025
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: this idea took three sessions to land; recently done - groups may ask whether SB36 covers what they want or leaves gaps (it is limited to over-appropriated basins and depends on funding).

theme_scorecards.label: Courts and oversight of water decisions
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: first committee (3); two got one-chamber floor wins in 2023-2025 and died in the second house (82:AB387 26-14; 83:SB31 15-6)
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: 0-for-6 across four sessions, but the last two attempts got further than the first four - movement without a finish.

theme_scorecards.label: Data centers, big water users, and tax deals
theme_scorecards.typical_stop: bills that would restrain data centers or big tax breaks died early or at the wire; bills that helped them passed
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: direction matters in this theme. Helping large users: 4 for 4 (80:SB547, 80:AB400, 81:AB66, 82:AB261). Restraining them: 0 for 5 (83:AB77, 82:SB394, 82:SB429, 81:AB449, and 83:SB364 which stalled). The one water-cooling bill (83:AB385) tried to protect evaporative cooling from local bans and died in its first committee.

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Create a statewide water study group / plan modeled on the Economic Forum - technical experts who inventory supply and report to the legislature.
proposal_reality_cards.record: Filed twice, finished zero times. 2019 SB499 (new state water planning advisory board) died in its first committee. 2025 SB143 (interim committee review of water conservation plus a turf study) passed the Senate 21-0 and died in the Assembly. No Economic Forum-style water body exists in statute.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Fully a state-legislature lever. The legislature's own interim committees did study data-center water/energy in 2026 - a temporary version of what citizens describe.
open_questions: What changed between the 2019 and 2025 failures?
Citizens' stated reason is that the legislature meets every other year - does an interim body answer that or duplicate existing interim committees?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Statewide guardrails on data centers: closed-loop cooling only, no evaporative cooling, pay for their own power; stop the county-by-county race to the bottom.
proposal_reality_cards.record: No bill has ever proposed state water or cooling rules for data centers. Bills helping large users went 4-for-4 (80:SB547, 80:AB400, 81:AB66, 82:AB261). Bills restraining their tax deals went 0-for-5 (83:AB77, 82:SB394, 82:SB429 vetoed, 81:AB449, 83:SB364). The one cooling bill (83:AB385) pushed the opposite way - blocking local cooling-tower bans - and died in first committee.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: The citizens' jurisdictional complaint is accurate: today cooling rules are local (SNWA moratorium is a water-authority rule; TRIC has its own water company). A statewide rule would be new statutory ground. 2026 interim study feeds the 2027 session.
open_questions: Which committee owns a 2027 bill - Natural Resources (water) or Revenue (tax deals)?
Closed-loop cooling raises electricity use - who owns the power half?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Audit and inventory water, fix over-allocation, digitize the records - the state knows about half its basins and was still on paper books in 2021.
proposal_reality_cards.record: Citizens said such bills 'keep getting shut down in committee' - the record agrees. 2025 AB485 would have funded basin water budgets, monitoring, digitization of Division records, and staff to adjudicate old claims; it died in its first committee. What did pass: 2019 AB95 (groundwater basin studies), 2023 SB113 (critical management areas, domestic-well meters), 2025 AB577 (money for the water agency's new website and database), 2025 SB36 (buy-and-retire over-allocated rights).
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Fully state-level: the Division of Water Resources' funding and duties are set by statute and budget.
open_questions: AB485 packaged funding + digitization + adjudication staff. Which piece drew the opposition?
Does AB577's database money cover what citizens mean by digitization, or just IT replacement?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Meter water use and use the data to catch leaks and overuse - metered water means people use less.
proposal_reality_cards.record: No bill has proposed a statewide metering mandate. Closest moves, all enacted: domestic wells in stressed basins can keep pumping 0.5 acre-feet only if the owner installs a meter (80:AB95, extended by 82:SB113); water suppliers must audit and report water losses (82:AB191); supplier conservation plans must address leaks (80:AB163).
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Rate metering of homes and businesses is a utility/local decision today (citizens' flat-rate complaint sits with cities and utilities); the legislature has only touched wells and supplier reporting.
open_questions: Which flat-rate systems remain, and are they city, utility, or district decisions?
Would a state mandate fund retrofits or just require them?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Stronger enforcement and steeper, escalating fines for water waste.
proposal_reality_cards.record: No bill on water-waste fines. The nearest law: 80:AB537 (enacted) raised civil penalties for environmental violations - pollution and public-water-system laws, not lawn watering. Watering-day enforcement and waste fines are utility/city rules today.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Citizens' own complaint - cities can't afford water monitors - is a funding question the legislature has never taken up for water enforcement.
open_questions: Are escalating fines a statute question or a utility-tariff question in each region?
Who would fund enforcement staff?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Incentivize heavy and business users to adopt water-saving technology - money talks.
proposal_reality_cards.record: No state incentive program for business water saving exists. Closest, both enacted: 81:SB283 let local improvement districts finance water-efficiency projects; 82:AB261 made the state economic development plan address efficient water use.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: State-level lever exists (GOED programs, improvement districts); it has been used for energy far more than water.
open_questions: Would an incentive tier ride on existing GOED machinery or need a new program?
What did SB283 districts actually finance so far?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Attach public-benefit conditions to tax incentives, paid as reimbursement at the end once metrics are hit - today companies get the keys up front.
proposal_reality_cards.record: The reimbursement-at-the-end design has never been filed. Every attempt to condition or cap the existing up-front abatements failed: 83:AB77 (grounds to deny; first committee), 82:SB394 (cap at $500k; passed Senate 14-7, died in Assembly Revenue), 82:SB429 (family-leave condition; vetoed), 81:AB449 (tighten agreements; first committee), 83:SB364 (stalled).
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Pure state lever (NRS 274/360/701A abatements). The vetoed bill shows the governor's desk is part of the path, not just the committees.
open_questions: SB394 passed the Senate before dying - is the blocker the idea or Assembly Revenue?
Is a reimbursement design different enough to change the coalition?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Restrictions on new and existing growth - watering days, decorative-lawn bans, moratoriums; limits may have to get more severe.
proposal_reality_cards.record: The strongest precedent in the record: 81:AB356 banned decorative Colorado River grass in southern Nevada, 21-0 and 42-0. But the follow-ons stalled: 82:SB169 (conservation/climate in local master plans) was vetoed; 83:SB143 (turf study) died in the Assembly. The SNWA evaporative-cooling moratorium is a local rule, not a statute. Land-split water dedication (80:SB250) passed 39-0.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Split lever: the legislature acted on grass; watering days and moratoriums have stayed local so far.
open_questions: AB356 applied only to the Colorado River service area - is a statewide version a fairness fix or a new regional fight?
What did the 2023 veto signal for planning mandates?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Fairness across users and regions - no carve-outs, water doesn't stop at county lines, be scientific about it.
proposal_reality_cards.record: The two bills closest to 'treat water as one statewide, scientific resource' both failed: 80:AB51 (State Engineer rules to manage all water sources together) died in first committee; 82:AB387 (best available science mandate) passed the Assembly 26-14 and died in the Senate. Meanwhile the enacted record is regional by design: the grass ban covers only southern Nevada; northern and southern dedication rules differ.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: The tension citizens named - my-county-first voting - is visible in the record as repeated committee deaths for statewide-standard bills.
open_questions: Is the Senate the consistent wall for science-standard bills, and why?
Which carve-outs are statutory vs. local practice?

proposal_reality_cards.citizen_statement: Bring your own water - require developers and heavy users to import their own supply and add it to the cost.
proposal_reality_cards.record: No statewide bring-your-own-water statute has been filed. Closest enacted law: 80:SB250 (39-0) on dedicating water rights before land can be split into parcels. In practice northern Nevada already works this way - developers must buy rights and dedicate them plus an 11% drought reserve - but that is a water-authority rule (TMWA), not state law; southern Nevada works differently under Lake Mead rules.
proposal_reality_cards.venue_note: Citizens' fairness question (self-supplied users still use shared pipes) is a rate/tariff question that sits with utilities and the PUC today.
open_questions: Would a statewide rule copy the northern model, and what happens where there is no market to buy rights?
Who prices the use of shared transmission?

people_and_process_signals.committee_vs_person_sponsored: 58 of 108 policy bills came from committees; 50 from named lawmakers.

frequent_primaries.name: Sen. Pete Goicoechea
frequent_primaries.note: groundwater, districts, retirement of rights; some enacted, several stalled

frequent_primaries.name: Asm. Howard Watts
frequent_primaries.note: conservation and clean-water bills across all four sessions

people_and_process_signals.committee_chokepoints: Of 27 policy bills that died in their first committee, 15 died in a Natural Resources committee. Assembly Revenue stopped both data-center abatement-restraint bills. Money committees (Finance/Ways & Means) stopped 2 appropriations.
people_and_process_signals.veto_watch: 2023 produced 3 vetoes in this set (82:AB97, 82:SB169, 82:SB429) - the governor became a live stop for planning mandates and abatement conditions.

high_support_non_enactments.note: groundwater boards; died at session end
resolution; passed but is only a request
abatement cap; died in Assembly Revenue
best available science; died in Senate

recent_enactments_watchlist.note: state account to buy and retire water rights in over-pumped basins - recently done; groups may ask whether funding and scope match the need
public notice for pollution permits (cross-party sponsors)
simpler rate changes for small water utilities (with 83:SB326)

deliberation_prompts: Five of the ten citizen statements have never been filed as a bill in this record (data-center water rules, statewide metering, water-waste fines, reimbursement-style abatements, bring-your-own-water). Unworkable - or unasked?
The records-and-audit bill (2025 AB485) died in its first committee, exactly the pattern citizens described. What did that committee hear, and from whom?
An expert water body failed in 2019 (first committee) and 2025 (second house). What changed between the two tries, and what still blocks it?
Tax-break conditions failed five ways, but 2023 SB394 first passed the Senate 14-7. Is the blocker the idea or Assembly Revenue's calendar?
The grass ban passed with zero no votes - and applies only to southern Nevada. Is a statewide version a fairness fix or a new regional fight?
Metering, waste fines, and watering days are mostly utility and city decisions today. Which parts of these ideas need a state law at all?
Three 2023 bills died by veto. How does the governor's desk change what 'realistic' means for planning mandates and abatement conditions?
15 of 27 first-committee deaths happened in a Natural Resources committee. Who sits there now, and what have they advanced before?
data_limits_carried_forward: Discovery is keyword search plus the LCB subject index - broad, not provably complete.
The record shows where bills stopped, never why.
Committee Yea votes are inferred from membership minus recorded Nay/Absent (marked in source).
Party labels from official NELIS rosters; 100% of ballot rows matched.
The 2026 interim data-center study is context from official documents, not part of the bill record.
